title: How Everything in a Medieval Castle Worked, from Its Moats to Its Dungeons
description:
in Architecture, History |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Very few of us have ever set foot near a genôÙuine medieval casôÙtle, espeôÙci
body:
in Architecture, History |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Very few of us have ever set foot near a genôÙuine medieval casôÙtle, espeôÙcialôÙly if we donãt hapôÙpen to live in Europe. Yet pracôÙtiôÙcalôÙly all of us still, here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry, refer with some freôÙquenôÙcy to their comôÙpoôÙnents in our everyôÙday speech. When we invoke moats, drawôÙbridges, dunôÙgeons, and even catôÙaôÙpults, we almost always do so metaphorôÙiôÙcalôÙly ã assumôÙing weãre not active memôÙbers of a hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal re-creôÙation sociôÙety ã yet we also have no probôÙlem seeôÙing them before our mindãs eye with what feels like perôÙfect clarôÙiôÙty. The difôÙfiôÙculôÙty comes if we attempt to inteôÙgrate all of those images, absorbed hapôÙhazôÙardôÙly from folk tales and popôÙuôÙlar culôÙture, into a funcôÙtionôÙing whole.
The fact of the matôÙter is that peoôÙple in the MidôÙdle Ages realôÙly did live and work in casôÙtles, and occaôÙsionôÙalôÙly had to defend them, or indeed attack them. Using a 3D-renôÙdered repliôÙca conôÙstructôÙed to reflect how those strucôÙtures were built in the fragôÙmentôÙed Europe of the eleventh through fourôÙteenth cenôÙturies after the fall of the CarôÙolinôÙgian Empire, the DeconôÙstructôÙed video aboveô explains everyôÙthing about how they worked in the span of about twenôÙty minôÙutes.
This tour begins with the barôÙbiôÙcan: not the celôÙeôÙbratôÙed BruôÙtalôÙist comôÙplex in LonôÙdon, but the exteôÙriôÙor forôÙtiôÙfied pasôÙsage ãdesigned to expose attackôÙers to defenôÙsive fire before they even reach the main gate.ã And it only gets hardôÙer for would-be casôÙtle capôÙtors from there.
ParaôÙpets with cutouts through which archers could fire their arrows, the moat that made underôÙminôÙing (a term comôÙmon enough in modôÙern lanôÙguage that few now recôÙogôÙnize its oriôÙgins) next to imposôÙsiôÙble, the drawôÙbridge that could be pulled up, the walls slantôÙed to repel batôÙterôÙing rams, the spiked portcullisôÙes that could be slammed down: these are just a few of the myrôÙiôÙad defensôÙes that made invadersã lives difôÙfiôÙcult ã and, in many casôÙes, short, espeôÙcialôÙly when ãmurôÙder holesã were involved. (Now thereãs a term just waitôÙing for incluôÙsion in our lexôÙiôÙcon.) The examôÙple conôÙstructôÙed here repôÙreôÙsents the zenith of casôÙtle design, the culôÙmiôÙnaôÙtion of an evoôÙluôÙtionôÙary process that began in the tenth cenôÙtuôÙry with a strucôÙture called the motte and baiôÙley: a term that, if you donãt already know it from othôÙer conôÙtexts, you probôÙaôÙbly just donãt do enough verôÙbal batôÙtle on the interôÙnet.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How To Build a 13th-CenôÙtuôÙry CasôÙtle, Using Only AuthenôÙtic Medieval Tools & TechôÙniques
Behold a 21st-CenôÙtuôÙry Medieval CasôÙtle Being Built with Only Tools & MateôÙriôÙals from the MidôÙdle Ages
The TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy That Brought Down Medieval CasôÙtles and Changed the MidôÙdle Ages
How Medieval CatheôÙdrals Were Built WithôÙout SciôÙence, or Even MathôÙeôÙmatôÙics
The Roman ColosôÙseôÙum DeconôÙstructôÙed: 3D AniôÙmaôÙtion Reveals the HidôÙden TechôÙnolôÙoôÙgy That PowôÙered Romeãs Great AreôÙna
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Discover the Retirement Home for Elderly Musicians Created by Giuseppe Verdi: Created in 1899, It Still Lives On Today
description:
in Life, Music |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Among my works, the one I like best is the Home that I have had built in Milan for ac
body:
in Life, Music |   	April 2nd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Among my works, the one I like best is the Home that I have had built in Milan for accomôÙmoôÙdatôÙing old singers not favored by forôÙtune, or who, when they were young did not posôÙsess the virtue of savôÙing. Poor and dear comôÙpanôÙions of my life!ô 
ãGiuseppe VerôÙdi
Is there a remôÙeôÙdy for the isoôÙlaôÙtion of old age?
What about the jolôÙly fraôÙterôÙniôÙty and comôÙpetôÙiôÙtiveôÙness of an art colôÙlege dorm, as enviôÙsioned by opera comôÙposôÙer Giuseppe VerôÙdi?
ShortôÙly before his death, the comôÙposôÙer donatôÙed all royôÙalôÙties from his operas to the conôÙstrucôÙtion and adminôÙisôÙtraôÙtion of a luxôÙuôÙriôÙous retreat for retired musiôÙcians, designed by his librettistãs brothôÙer, archiôÙtect CamilôÙlo Boito.
ComôÙpletôÙed in 1899, Casa VerôÙdi still serves elderôÙly musiôÙcians todayãup to 60 at a time. ResôÙiôÙdents of Casa VerôÙdiô include alumôÙnae of the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan Opera and the RoyôÙal Opera House. Guests have worked alongôÙside such notaôÙbles as Chet BakôÙer and Maria Callas.
ComôÙpeôÙtiôÙtion for resôÙiôÙdenôÙtial slots is stiff. To qualôÙiôÙfy, one must have been a proôÙfesôÙsionôÙal musiôÙcian or music teacher. Those selectôÙed enjoy room, board, and medôÙical treatôÙment in addiôÙtion to, writes The New York Times,ô ãaccess to conôÙcerts, music rooms, 15 pianos, a large organ, harps, drum sets and the comôÙpaôÙny of their peers.ã MusiôÙcal proôÙgramôÙming is as conôÙstant as the fine view of Verdiãs grave.
DinôÙing tables are named in honôÙor of Verdiãs works. Those inclined to worôÙship do so in a chapel named for SanôÙta CecilôÙia, the patron saint of musiôÙcians.
PracôÙtice rooms are alive with the sound of music and critôÙiôÙcism. As Casa Verdiãs music therôÙaôÙpist told the FinanôÙcial Times, ãThey are very comôÙpetôÙiôÙtive: they are all priôÙma donôÙnas.ã
When memôÙoôÙry fails, resôÙiôÙdents can tune in to such docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaries as actor Dustin Hoffmanãs Toscaãs Kiss, below


	

Get a peek inside Verdiãs retireôÙment home for artists, comôÙpliôÙments of Urban SketchôÙers here.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2018.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How a Dutch ãDemenôÙtia VilôÙlageã Improves QualôÙiôÙty of Life with IntenôÙtionôÙal Design
Meet Nadia Boulanger, ãThe Most InfluôÙenôÙtial Teacher Since Socrates,ã Who MenôÙtored Philip Glass, Leonard BernôÙstein, Aaron CopôÙland, QuinôÙcy Jones & OthôÙer LegôÙends
How Music Can AwakôÙen Patients with Alzheimerãs and DemenôÙtia
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙday is an author, illusôÙtraôÙtor, and theôÙater makôÙer in NYC.

title: How James Cameron Shot Titanicãs Hugely Complex Sinking Scene
description:
in Film, History |   	April 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The dark arts of ãHolôÙlyôÙwood accountôÙingã make it difôÙfiôÙcult to deterôÙmine film b
body:
in Film, History |   	April 1st, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

The dark arts of ãHolôÙlyôÙwood accountôÙingã make it difôÙfiôÙcult to deterôÙmine film budôÙgets with preôÙciôÙsion. But accordôÙing to reaôÙsonôÙable reckôÙonôÙings, James Cameron may have directôÙed not just one but sevôÙerôÙal of the most expenôÙsive movies of all time. The underôÙwaôÙter sci-fi specôÙtaôÙcle that was The Abyss necesôÙsiôÙtatôÙed one of the biggest proôÙducôÙtion budôÙgets of the eightôÙies, but it looked straight off PoverôÙty Row when comôÙpared to Cameronãs next project just two years latôÙer. TerôÙmiôÙnaôÙtor 2: JudgôÙment Day was the first film to cost more than $100 milôÙlion; True Lies, his next Arnold SchwarzenegôÙger vehiôÙcle, could have cost as much as $120 milôÙlion. What chalôÙlenge remained for Cameronô at that point? Why,ô re-creôÙatôÙing the most famous shipôÙwreck in hisôÙtoôÙry.
Such an improbôÙaôÙble-soundôÙing ambiôÙtion didôÙnãt come out of nowhere. FasôÙciôÙnatôÙed with theô TitanôÙicô since childôÙhood, Cameron evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly found himôÙself able to make mulôÙtiôÙple expeôÙdiôÙtions of his own to its final restôÙing place in deep-sea subôÙmersibles. He wasôÙnãt just well placed to gathôÙer the inforôÙmaôÙtion necôÙesôÙsary to bring it back to life on screen, but also to impleôÙment and indeed develôÙop the techôÙniques to film it believôÙably, powôÙerôÙfulôÙly, and with a high degree of hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal accuôÙraôÙcy.
It perôÙhaps does Cameron a disôÙserôÙvice to refer to him only as a filmôÙmakôÙer, since throughôÙout his career heãs disôÙplayed just as much the mind of an engiôÙneer, charôÙacôÙterôÙized by the willôÙingôÙness to make his own techôÙnoôÙlogôÙiôÙcal advanceôÙments in the serôÙvice of bringôÙing his vision to the screen. You can get some insight into that mind at work in the StuôÙdio Binder video above on how he directôÙedô the TitanôÙicãs sinkôÙing scene.


	

TitanôÙic cost $200 milôÙlion, more than the ship herôÙself. In 1997, that was an eye-waterôÙing sum, but givôÙen the movieãs evenôÙtuôÙal take of $2.264 bilôÙlion, it seems monôÙey well spent. A non-trivôÙial amount of those profôÙits came from viewôÙers who bought a tickôÙet ã again and again, in some casôÙes ã expressôÙly to see their favorite heartôÙthrob. But Cameron must have known full well that most movieôÙgoôÙers turned up to see the ship go down; everyôÙthing thus rode on that one hour of the filmãs 195-minute runôÙtime. Its unpreceôÙdentôÙedôÙly comôÙplex shoot involved, among othôÙer things, hunôÙdreds of stunt perôÙformôÙers and extras,ô the latôÙest in CGI tools, and a 775-foot-long repliôÙca of theô TitanôÙic installed in a cusôÙtom-built seaôÙside set in MexôÙiôÙco. The scene, as well as the film that conôÙtains it, holds up nearôÙly thirôÙty years latôÙer in part due to this comôÙbiôÙnaôÙtion of digôÙiôÙtal and anaôÙlog effects, a fusion of almost experôÙiôÙmenôÙtalôÙly cutôÙting-edge digôÙiôÙtal techôÙnolôÙoôÙgy and old-fashôÙioned, thorôÙoughôÙly anaôÙlog movie magôÙic ã someôÙthing Cameron underôÙstands just as well as he does underôÙsea exploôÙration.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The FasôÙciôÙnatôÙing EngiôÙneerôÙing of the TitanôÙic: How the Great Ocean LinôÙer Was Built
Watch 80 MinôÙutes of NevôÙer-Released Footage ShowôÙing the WreckôÙage of the TitanôÙic (1986)
The First Full 3D Scan of the TitanôÙic, Made of More Than 700,000 Images CapôÙturôÙing the Wreckãs Every Detail
TitanôÙic SurôÙvivor InterôÙviews: What It Was Like to Flee the SinkôÙing LuxôÙuôÙry LinôÙer
Watch the TitanôÙic Sink in Real-Time
How the TitanôÙic Sank: James Cameronãs New CGI AniôÙmaôÙtion
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Discover the First Horror & Fantasy Magazine, Der Orchideengarten, and Its Bizarre Artwork (1919ã1921)
description: From the 18th cenôÙtuôÙry onward, the genôÙres of GothôÙic horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy have flourôÙished, and with them the senôÙsuôÙalôÙly visôÙcerôÙal images n
body:
From the 18th cenôÙtuôÙry onward, the genôÙres of GothôÙic horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy have flourôÙished, and with them the senôÙsuôÙalôÙly visôÙcerôÙal images now comôÙmonôÙplace in film, TV, and comôÙic books. These genôÙres perôÙhaps reached their aesôÙthetôÙic peak in the 19th cenôÙtuôÙry with writôÙers like Edgar Allan Poe and illusôÙtraôÙtors like GusôÙtave Dore. But it was in the earôÙly twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry that a more popôÙulist subôÙgenre truôÙly came into its own: ãweird ficôÙtion,ã a term H.P. LoveôÙcraft used to describe the pulpy brand of superôÙnatôÙurôÙal horôÙror codôÙiôÙfied in the pages of AmerôÙiôÙcan fanôÙtaôÙsy and horôÙror magôÙaôÙzine Weird Talesãfirst pubôÙlished in 1923. (And still going strong!)

A preôÙcurôÙsor to EC Comicsã many lurid titles, Weird Tales is often conôÙsidôÙered the definôÙiôÙtive earôÙly twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry venue for weird ficôÙtion and illusôÙtraôÙtion.
But we need only look back a few years and to anothôÙer conôÙtiôÙnent to find an earôÙliôÙer pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtion, servôÙing GerôÙman-speakôÙing fansãDer OrchideenôÙgarten (ãThe GarôÙden of Orchidsã), the very first horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzine, which ran 51 issues from JanôÙuôÙary 1919 to NovemôÙber 1921.

The magôÙaôÙzine feaôÙtured work from its ediôÙtors Karl Hans Strobl and Alfons von CzibulôÙka, from betôÙter-known conôÙtemôÙpoôÙraries like H.G. Wells and Karel Capek, and from foreôÙfaôÙthers like DickôÙens, Pushkin, Guy de MauôÙpasôÙsant, Poe, Voltaire, WashôÙingôÙton IrvôÙing, Nathaniel Hawthorne, and othôÙers. ãAlthough two issues of Der OrchideenôÙgarten were devotôÙed to detecôÙtive stoôÙries,ã writes 50 Watts, ãand one to erotôÙic stoôÙries about cuckôÙolds, it was a genôÙuine fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzine.ã And it was also a gallery of bizarre and unusuôÙal artôÙwork.

50 Watts quotes from Franz Rottensteinerãs descripôÙtion of the magazineãs art, which rangedô ãfrom repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtions of medieval woodôÙcuts to the work of masôÙters of the macabre such as GusôÙtave Dore or Tony JohanôÙnot, to conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary GerôÙman artists like Rolf von HoerôÙschelôÙmann, Otto LennekoôÙgel, Karl RitôÙter, HeinôÙrich Kley, or Alfred Kubin.ã These artists creôÙatôÙed the covôÙers and illusôÙtraôÙtions you see here, and many more you can see atô 50 Watts, the black sun, and John Coulthartãs {feuilôÙleton}.

ãWhat strikes me about these black-and-white drawôÙings,ã like the dense, frenôÙzied pen-and-ink scene above, Coulthart comôÙments, ãis how difôÙferôÙent they are in tone to the pulp magôÙaôÙzines which folôÙlowed shortôÙly after in AmerôÙiôÙca and elseôÙwhere. Theyãre at once far more adult and freôÙquentôÙly more origôÙiôÙnal than the GothôÙic clichûˋs which padded out Weird Tales and lessôÙer titles for many years.ã Indeed, though the forôÙmat may be simôÙiôÙlar to its sucôÙcesôÙsors, Der OrchideenôÙgartenãs covôÙers show the influôÙence of SurôÙreôÙalôÙism, ãsome are almost ExpresôÙsionôÙist in style,ã and many of the illusôÙtraôÙtions show ãa disôÙtinct Goya influôÙence.ã

PopôÙuôÙlar fanôÙtaôÙsy and horôÙror illusôÙtraôÙtion has often leaned more toward the soft-porn of sevôÙenôÙties airôÙbrushed vans, pulp-novôÙel covôÙers, or the grisôÙlyô kitsch of the comics. RotôÙtenôÙsteinôÙer writes in his 1978 FanôÙtaôÙsy Book that this ãlarge-forôÙmat magôÙaôÙzineãÎ must sureôÙly rank as one of the most beauôÙtiôÙful fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzines ever pubôÙlished.ã Itãs hard to argue with that assessôÙment. View, read (in GerôÙman), and downôÙload origôÙiôÙnal scans of the magazineãs first sevôÙerôÙal issues over on this PrinceôÙton site.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How Georges Mûˋliû´sô A Trip to the Moonô Became the First Sci-Fi Film & Changed CinôÙeôÙma ForôÙevôÙer (1902)
Read MarôÙgaret Cavendishãsô The BlazôÙing World: The First Sci-Fi NovôÙel WritôÙten By a Woman (1666)
The First Work of SciôÙence FicôÙtion: Read Lucianãs 2nd-CenôÙtuôÙry Space TravôÙelôÙogueô A True StoôÙry
Free: 356 Issues ofô Galaxy, the GroundôÙbreakôÙing 1950s SciôÙence FicôÙtion MagôÙaôÙzine
Josh Jonesô is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC. FolôÙlow him atô @jdmagness

title: Stitch Your Favorite Destinations with Jake Henzler's 'Knit the City'
description:
All images courtesy of Jake Henzler and David & Charles Publishing, shared with permission
April 1, 2026


When we visit major hubs like Copenhagen o
body:
All images courtesy of Jake Henzler and David & Charles Publishing, shared with permission
April 1, 2026


When we visit major hubs like Copenhagen or Paris, we often take a lot of photos and make sure to grab a little souvenir as a memento of our visit. How better to remember the architecture and the feel of the city? Well, fiber designer Jake Henzler, a.k.a. Boy Knits World, figures you can stitch those memories into something much cozier than a postcard or a keychain.
Forthcoming from David & Charles Publishing, Henzlerãs book Knit the City highlights buildings around the world through a series of building block-like patterns. Using a modular system, details like gables and windows can be switched up to create your own unique facades. Then itãs up to you to choose the colors youãd like to use. The blocks can then be stitched together to create a blanket, pillow covers, or any other application you can come up with.
Knit the City is slated for release on May 5. Pre-order your copy in the Colossal Shop. You can also find Henzlerãs patterns on Ravelry.

schema.headline: Stitch Your Favorite Destinations with Jake Henzler's 'Knit the City'

title: Symbiotic Communion Flourishes in Laura Berger's Expansive Paintings
description: Chicago-based artist Laura Berger continues her explorations of communion in a suite of staggering paintings that place her signature minimal figu
body:
Chicago-based artist Laura Berger continues her explorations of communion in a suite of staggering paintings that place her signature minimal figures in intimate fellowship with one another and the earth. Spanning six feet wide, the monumental works layer limbs and landscapes, as nude bodies merge with waves, flowers, and sun-strewn clouds. 
Berger frequently gestures toward past experiences and the spiritual realm, particularly focusing on the ways time fogs the clarity of memory and how transformation emerges from myriad circumstances. This body of work expands on the themes of interdependence, too, as she renders her figures in varying states of translucence as they swim through a cresting wave or lounge atop pink petals.
ãThe Skyã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
These pieces will be on view from April 17 to May 23 at Kristin Hjellegjerde Galleryô in West Palm Beach. Until then, explore more of the artistãs work on Instagram.
Detail of ãWomen Living Peacefully Under The Mountainã (2026)
ãSpring Tideã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
ãWe Are Sleeping In The Gardenã (2026), oil on canvas, 38 x 72 inches
Detail of ãSpring Tideã (2026)

schema.headline: Symbiotic Communion Flourishes in Laura Berger's Expansive Paintings

title: A Line of Mural Wallpapers from Astek Celebrates 'Eterna Nouveau'
description:
All images courtesy of Astek, shared with permission
April 2, 2026


Living in a high-rise apartment or a house with a small yard comes with the disa
body:
All images courtesy of Astek, shared with permission
April 2, 2026


Living in a high-rise apartment or a house with a small yard comes with the disadvantage of not having access to garden space. Fortunately, fine wallpaper manufacturer Astek has a way to bring beautiful blooms indoors. The companyãs collection of dreamy floral mural designs called Eterna Nouveau is conceived as a reinterpretation of the Art Nouveau movement of the early 20th century, which historically flourished in Europe and emphasized nature-inspired motifs like flowers and birds.
Eterna Nouveauãs arching, sinuous stems and leaves nod to its namesake styleãs characteristic ãwhiplashã lines. ãAquavita,ã for example, features lilies and other water plants that illustrates life both above and below the surface. And ãCarnivoriaã celebrates more unusual plants, like Venus fly traps. A variety of colorways emphasize the designsã bold forms and delicate metallic outlines.

The motifs were first created by hand, then digitized so that they can be sized up or down to fit custom spaces. Printed to order, the colors and shapes can even be customized for special projects. See more on Astekãs Instagram.

schema.headline: A Line of Mural Wallpapers from Astek Celebrates 'Eterna Nouveau'
